title: Triangle inequality for real absolute value
informal_statement: For every pair of real numbers a, b, the absolute value of a + b is at most |a| + |b| (the triangle inequality for real absolute value).
informal_proof_idea: abs_cases splits each of a, b, and a+b into its sign case (|x| = x with 0 <= x, or |x| = -x with x < 0); after the 2x2x2 = 8-way case split every remaining goal is a purely linear fact about a, b, |a|, |b|, |a+b| given their sign-determined values, closed uniformly by nlinarith.

review.review_basis: repo self-review; kernel-verified through the tracked proof-search loop

notes: Proof authority: Lean kernel via the tracked proof-search loop (episode 0c084674), kernel_verified on the first attempt after an earlier problem_create was missing Mathlib.Data.Real.Basic in problem_imports (its absence produced 'failed to synthesize instance of type class HAdd/Lattice/AddCommGroup R' -- ℝ's algebraic/order structure instances live there, not in Mathlib.Algebra.Order.Group.Abs alone). Fills an L0 gap in a domain that was skewed toward L2_olympiad content (15 of 19 packets); this is the domain's first genuinely elementary absolute-value packet -- reverse_triangle.v1 (the reverse triangle inequality) was filed at L2 despite being comparably basic.
